coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from green jujube as chemosensor for tetracycline detection
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Liao, Shihua
Bai, Yuefeng
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: The detection of tetracycline antibiotics in environmental waters is crucial due to their widespread use, persistence, and potential toxicity. Herein, highly fluorescent blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a facile, green solvothermal approach using green jujube as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen dopant. The B-CDs exhibited an exceptional quantum yield of 24.7% in ethanol along with excitation-dependent photoluminescence from surface defects. Remarkably, the B-CDs displayed high selectivity and sensitivity toward tetracycline antibiotics through static quenching and inner filter effects. A linear correlation between fluorescence quenching and tetracycline concentration enabled quantitative detection down to 0.061 μM. The feasibility of using B-CDs for antibiotic sensing in real waters was validated by spike-recovery experiments achieving 95.4%–100.2% recoveries. The findings provide insights into natural product-derived carbon nanomaterials while offering an eco-friendly sensing platform with potential for on-site antibiotic monitoring and environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Quantum yield
Solvothermal method
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: As a fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the B-CDs synthesis.

Controlled experiment. (a) The effect of different carbon sources on the fluorescence properties of carbon dots. Comparisons of (b) fluorescence spectra, (c) fluorescence intensities, (d) maximum emission wavelengths B-CDs and carbon dots synthesized by jujube and ethylenediamine alone.

Structural characterization. (a) TEM image of G-CDs. Insets are the corresponding HRTEM image at top left corner and particle size distribution at top right corner. (b) infrared spectra. (c) XPS survey spectrum. (d-f) XPS spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s at high resolution.

Optical properties of B-CDs. (a) three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum and its (b) two-dimensional projection. (c) UV-Vis absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (blue), emission spectrum (multi-color). (d) Emission spectra and (e) normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
Effects of different solvents on the luminescence properties of B-CDs. (a) Fitting curves of integrated fluorescence intensity to absorbance and (b) quantum yields of B-CDs in different solvents.
The stability of B-CDs. (a) The dependence of B-CDs fluorescence intensity on NaCl concentration. (b) The evolution of fluorescence intensity of B-CDs with the irradiation time of UV lamp.

B-CDs as a chemosensor for the detection of tetracycline. (a) The effect of different ions and antibiotics on the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs. (b) The effect of tetracycline concentration on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs. (c) The relationship between the concentration of tetracycline and (F 0-F) / F 0.
Mechanism analysis of B-CDs for detecting tetracycline (a) UV absorption spectra of B-CDs, TC, and B-CDs+TC (b) The UV absorption spectrum of TC and the maximum excitation spectrum of B-CDs.
Comparison of quantum yields of B-CDs in this work with other blue emitting carbon dots in the literature.
Determination of tetracycline content in sewage.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from green jujube as chemosensor for tetracycline detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of tetracycline antibiotics in environmental waters is crucial due to their widespread use, persistence, and potential toxicity. Herein, highly fluorescent blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a facile, green solvothermal approach using green jujube as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen dopant. The B-CDs exhibited an exceptional quantum yield of 24.7% in ethanol along with excitation-dependent photoluminescence from surface defects. Remarkably, the B-CDs displayed high selectivity and sensitivity toward tetracycline antibiotics through static quenching and inner filter effects. A linear correlation between fluorescence quenching and tetracycline concentration enabled quantitative detection down to 0.061 μM. The feasibility of using B-CDs for antibiotic sensing in real waters was validated by spike-recovery experiments achieving 95.4%–100.2% recoveries. The findings provide insights into natural product-derived carbon nanomaterials while offering an eco-friendly sensing platform with potential for on-site antibiotic monitoring and environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) are a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that are widely used in medical and veterinary fields, primarily for bacterial infection treatment and as feed additives[1,2]. However, due to the difficulty in complete decomposition and removal [3,4] of TCs, their residues via bioaccumulation may pose threats to human health, e.g., liver toxicity and gastrointestinal disorders. Therefore, effective detection of TCs is of great importance. Conventional TC detection methods such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), paper chromatography and capillary electrophoresis [5–7], though widely used, usually require complicated operation procedures and expensive equipment. Given the limitations of these methods, exploring more convenient and efficient detection methods becomes the focus of research. In this aspect, fluorescence analysis methods, with the features of simple operation, fast response and high sensitivity, have become a promising simple method for anti-biotic detection[8].

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of luminescent material, possess advantages like high optical stability, low cost, low toxicity, good water solubility, simple preparation process, and excellent luminescent properties[9], showing huge application potential in areas like solar cells [10–12], sensors [13,14], optoelectronic devices[15,16] and bioimaging [17–20]. So far, many methods have been developed to synthesize CDs, but most of them have some drawbacks, e.g., complicated synthetic routes or the use of toxic/expensive solvents/precursors, not meeting the philosophy of environmental protection and sustainable development. Using natural products as carbon sources to prepare fluorescent CDs is green, efficient and cost-effective, with tunable and excellent performances. Naturally derived CDs usually have good biocompatibility and biodegradability with very low toxicity. Many natural product molecules inherently contain heterocycles or heteroatoms, which can effectively achieve heteroatom doping in CDs and optimize their physicochemical properties.

Green jujube is a kind of tropical and subtropical fruit rich in vitamin C, phosphorus, vitamin B, carotenoids, etc[21]. Previously our group has successfully synthesized a series of green CDs using other natural products such as Sichuan pepper [22], cordyceps militaris[23], bay leaf [24] and kaempferide[25], however, these CDs responded to alcohol detection and fluorescent ink application instead of antibiotic detection. In this work, the common, renewable and inexpensive green jujube was chosen as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source to prepare blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs) via a green hydrothermal approach. Owing to the high sensitivity and selectivity of B-CDs towards tetracycline induced fluorescence quenching, a detection method for tetracycline was established, which could be used for tetracycline detection in wastewater. This method features advantages like high sensitivity and selectivity, low cost and easy availability of materials, environmental friendliness, operational simplicity, and applicability for environmental water samples, showing potential for tetracycline detection in real water bodies. This research adopted a green, economical and facile strategy to synthesize CDs from renewable plant resources and applied them to antibiotic detection, not only holding significance for investigating natural product derived CDs, but also providing new insights into antibiotic detection.

The B-CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal approach using jujube fruit as the carbon source. In brief, 10 g of fresh jujube was blended and soaked in 200 mL of ethanol for 12 h. Then, 5 mL of the resulting juice was transferred into a tetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave, along with 600 mg of ethylenediamine, 100 mg of nitric acid, and 5 mL of ethanol. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min and subsequently heated at 180 °C for 5 h in an air blast drying box, followed by being cooled down to room temperature naturally to obtain the B-CDs solution. The synthetic procedures were illustrated as shown in Fig. 1.

To assess the influence of raw materials on the luminescent properties of carbon dots, various fruits and vegetables were chosen as carbon sources: green jujube, mulberry, glycyrrhiza uralensis seed, red bean, and pumpkin. Ethylenediamine was used as a nitrogen dopant to synthesize carbon dots. Furthermore, a controlled experiment was conducted to compare the luminescent performance of carbon dots synthesized using green jujube alone, ethylenediamine alone, and their combination under the same conditions.

According to literature[26], quinine sulfate has a quantum yield φ = 0.54 at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution. Using quinine sulfate as a standard reference, one can estimate the relative quantum yield of carbon dots using the following equation:
(1)
φ
U
=
φ
S
⋅
F
U
F
S
⋅
A
S
A
U
⋅
(
η
S
η
U
)
2
=
φ
S
⋅
k
U
k
S
⋅
(
η
S
η
U
)
2
Where φ is fluorescence quantum yield, F is integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance at excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index of solvent; subscripts S and U refer to standard sample and test sample, respectively. To avoid inner filter effects, absorbances were kept under 0.05 for all samples.

To investigate the impact of various carbon sources on the luminescent properties of carbon dots, diverse fruits and vegetables (green jujube, mulberry, glycyrrhiza uralensis seed, red bean, and pumpkin) were chosen as carbon sources, along with ethylenediamine as a nitrogen dopant, to synthesize carbon dots under identical conditions. Their fluorescence intensities are depicted in Fig. 2a, clearly demonstrating that B-CDs produced using green jujube and ethylenediamine exhibit the highest fluorescent intensity. To further validate the effect of the green jujube and ethylenediamine mixture on carbon dots luminescence, a group of control experiments was conducted, where carbon dots were synthesized using green jujube alone, ethylenediamine alone, and their combination. By comparing the emission spectra, fluorescent intensities, and maximum emission wavelengths of these resulted CDs (Fig. 2b-d), it is clear the combination of green jujube and ethylenediamine significantly enhances the fluorescent intensity of carbon dots, resulting in an approximate 90% increase. These observations suggest that the combination of green jujube and ethylenediamine effectively optimizes the luminescent properties of carbon dots, leading to the synthesis of B-CDs with more desirable luminescent performance.

The morphology, surface functional groups, and chemical composition of B-CDs were meticulously analyzed using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). TEM micrograph, as presented in Fig. 3a, have demonstrated the presence of carbon dot particles, which exhibit an average diameter of 3.5 nanometers. High-resolution TEM image reveals well-defined lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (100) lattice plane characteristic of graphitic carbon structures, aligning with previously reported findings[27]. The FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 3b, reveals distinctive absorption peaks: the peak around 3500 cm−1 corresponds to N-H or O-H stretching vibrations; the peak at 2364 cm−1 is attributed to CN stretching vibrations; the peak located at 1573 cm−1 indicates C-N stretching vibrations, and the peak at 1054 cm−1 signifies C-O stretching vibrations. These findings suggest that the synthesized B-CDs were successfully doped with nitrogen and likely contain water-soluble functional groups such as -NH2 and -OH. XPS provided further elemental analysis and valence state characterization of the B-CDs (Fig. 3c). The full survey scan shows the B-CDs consist of C, O, and N, at percentages of 62.75%, 16.72%, and 20.53%, respectively. High resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) can be deconvoluted into four peaks, attributable to C-C/CC (284.8 eV), C-N (286.0 eV), C-O (288.0 eV), and COOH (292.6 eV) moieties. The O1s peak at 532.2 eV is shown in Fig. 3e. Two peaks are observed in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3f), corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen (398.2 eV) and amino nitrogen (400.1 eV). The FTIR and XPS data consistently demonstrate the B-CDs possess abundant N and O-containing functional groups, conferring good water solubility.

Optical properties of the B-CDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectroscopy. The three-dimensional excitation-emission spectrum and its two-dimensional projection of the B-CDs are shown in Fig. 4a and b respectively, which clearly depict the main excitation region from 300 nm to 500 nm and the emission region from 400 nm to 600 nm. Fig. 4c shows UV–vis absorption spectrum as well as fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. It is clear that B-CDs exhibit a significant absorption band below approximately 400 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectrum. The maximum excitation peak is centered at 400 nm, corresponding to a maximum emission wavelength of 478 nm. Additionally, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission peak is 91 nm.

To investigate the photoluminescence mechanism of the B-CDs, we studied the excitation dependence of the emission spectra. Emission spectra of the B-CDs were measured under varied excitation wavelengths from 375 nm to 425 nm (Fig. 4d). The emission maxima shift with changing excitation wavelength. After normalization (Fig. 4e), the emission peaks do not overlap, demonstrating that the emission wavelength of the B-CDs is dependent of the excitation wavelength. Currently, there are several theories to explain the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs, namely size distribution and quantum confinement effects[28,29], excitation-dependent surface states[30,31], and molecular fluorophores[31,32]. While the carbon core of the B-CDs may exhibit quantum confinement effects, their surface states could also show dependence on the excitation wavelength. Functional groups on the surface of carbon dots might respond differently to light of different energies, absorbing and emitting differently when the excitation wavelength is changed. This means that different surface states could be excited with different wavelengths, resulting in varying emission characteristics. Therefore, the photoluminescence type of B-CDs may belong to Excitation-dependent Surface States.

Understanding the influence of solvents on the luminescent properties of carbon dots is critical for their applications. To quantitatively assess the effects of different solvents on the photoluminescence of carbon dots, the quantum yields of B-CDs were measured in several common laboratory solvents including water (WAT), methanol (MET), ethanol (ETH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The absorbance and fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs at varied concentrations were acquired in each solvent. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the integrated fluorescence intensity was fitted as a function of absorbance, and the slopes of the linear fits correlate with the quantum yields according to Eq. 1. Using quinine sulfate (REF) as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yields of B-CDs were determined as shown in Fig. 5b. Notably, B-CDs exhibited the highest quantum yield of 24.7% in ethanol. Table 1 compares the quantum yields of several reported blue-emitting carbon dots, demonstrating that the B-CDs synthesized in this work possess superior quantum efficiency over most blue carbon dots, which is advantageous for applications.

To verify the physicochemical stability of the B-CDs, the effects of salt concentration and time on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs (excited at 400 nm) were investigated. The salt tolerance of B-CDs was studied by measuring the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs solutions with consistent B-CDs concentrations but varying NaCl concentrations of 0, 100 mM, 200 mM, 300 mM, 400 mM, and 500 mM. As depicted in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs was not significantly impacted across the range of NaCl concentrations tested, demonstrating stable emission of B-CDs even under high salinity conditions. Additionally, the photostability of B-CDs was analyzed by continuously irradiating B-CDs solutions with UV light for 120 minutes and evaluating the temporal fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 6b. A slight decrease in fluorescence was observed over time, indicating acceptable photobleaching resistance of the B-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: As a fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots have demonstrated tremendous potential across diverse applications including fluorescent probes, bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, and photovoltaic devices. Here, we briefly introduce the utilization of B-CDs as a fluorescent probe for detecting tetracycline antibiotics. B-CDs solutions were treated with the tetracycline antibiotics ampicillin, gentamicin, amoxicillin, chlortetracycline and kanamycin, as well as common ions including Zn2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, K+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, and Ca2+, with all antibiotics and ions at concentrations of 37.5 μmol/L. The fluorescence intensity response of the B-CDs to each antibiotic or ion was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. As shown in Fig. 7a, among all the tested antibiotics and ions, only tetracyclines caused a substantial decrease in B-CDs fluorescence. This demonstrates the high selectivity and anti-interference capability of the B-CDs towards tetracyclines, indicating suitability as fluorescent probes for detecting tetracyclines in environmental water samples.

As shown in Fig. 7b, within the range of tetracycline concentrations from 0 to 0.1 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of tetracycline. The relationship between (F 0 -F)/F0 and the concentration of tetracycline exhibits a good linear correlation (F represents the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots after adding different concentrations of tetracycline, and F 0 is the blank value). As shown in Fig. 7c, the linear regression equation was determined to be (F 0-F)/F0 =0.09984C TC+0.03614, with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9914. The limit of detection reached 0.061 μmol/L, suggesting applicability for detecting tetracyclines in water samples.

To validate the feasibility of the method, the content of tetracycline in actual water samples (lake water) was detected, and spike recovery experiments were conducted. As shown in Table 2, tetracycline was not detected in the lake water, and the measured spike recovery rates ranged from 95.4% to 100.2%, with relative standard deviations between 2.2% and 2.9%.

To investigate the mechanism of tetracycline-induced fluorescence quenching of B-CDs, ultraviolet absorption spectra of solutions containing B-CDs, tetracycline (TC), and B-CDs + TC were compared. As shown in Fig. 8a, tetracycline absorption peaks (2) occur predominantly at ∼250 nm, ∼290 nm and ∼390 nm. Upon addition of tetracycline to B-CDs solutions, significant spectral changes were observed (3), likely due to formation of a nonfluorescent ground state complex between tetracycline and the cyclodextrin moieties of B-CDs. This static quenching mechanism arises from complex formation. Additionally, substantial spectral overlap can be noted in Fig. 8b between the tetracycline absorption spectrum and the maximum excitation peak wavelength of B-CDs emission. This indicates tetracycline induces inner filter effects that further quench B-CDs fluorescence. In summary, both static quenching via ground state complexation and inner filter phenomena contribute to tetracycline-mediated quenching of tailored B-CDs fluorescence. Elucidation of the dual underlying mechanisms reinforces the potential of fluorescently-labeled cyclodextrins for selective and sensitive tetracycline detection with minimal interference.

In this work, highly fluorescent B-CDs were successfully synthesized from the natural product green jujube via a facile, green solvothermal approach using ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source. The combination of jujube fruit and ethylenediamine effectively optimized the luminescent properties, resulting in B-CDs with a high quantum yield of 24.7% in ethanol. Detailed characterization revealed the B-CDs were nitrogen and oxygen doped, possessing good water solubility and excitation-dependent emission behavior arising from surface defect states. The as-prepared B-CDs exhibited excellent photostability, salt tolerance, and resistance to photobleaching, making them suitable for sensing applications. Remarkably, the B-CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity towards tetracycline antibiotics through static quenching and inner filter effects. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching and tetracycline concentration was established, enabling quantitative detection down to 0.061 μM. The spike recovery experiments in real water samples further validated the feasibility of this fluorescence method for tetracycline monitoring in environmental waters. The findings provide insights into natural product-derived carbon nanomaterials while offering a green, cost-effective approach for antibiotic detection with potential environmental applications.

Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jing Hu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yuefeng Bai: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shihua Liao: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.
